topics.title: Topic 1: Introduction to Java
topics.Topic Introduction:
Programming languages help humans communicate with computers.

Java was designed to:

Be easy to learn
Be secure
Be platform independent
Support large applications
Reduce system dependency
Java Architecture
JDK (Java Development Kit)

Provides tools required to develop Java applications.

Examples:

Compiler
Debugger
Documentation tools
JRE (Java Runtime Environment)

Provides the environment required to run Java applications.

JVM (Java Virtual Machine)

Responsible for executing Java bytecode.

Execution Flow
Java Source Code (.java)

↓
Compiler (javac)

↓
Bytecode (.class)

↓
JVM

↓
topics.Definition: Java is a high-level, object-oriented, platform-independent programming language developed by Sun Microsystems in 1995.
topics.Output:
Welcome to Java
Java is Platform Independent
Learning Java is Fun
topics.Common Mistakes:
Mistake 1
System.out.println("Hello")

Missing semicolon.

Correct:

System.out.println("Hello");
Mistake 2

Wrong file name.

If class name is:

public class Main

File name must be:

Main.java
topics.Best Practices:
Use meaningful class names.
Follow Java naming conventions.
Keep code properly indented.
Add comments when necessary.
topics.Key Points:
Java is platform independent.
JVM executes bytecode.
JDK is used for development.
JRE is used for execution.
Java follows WORA.
topics.Problem Statement:
Write a Java program that prints the following information exactly as shown:

Welcome to Java
Java is Platform Independent
Learning Java is Fun
topics.Output Format: Print the three lines exactly as specified.
topics.Sample Output:
Welcome to Java
Java is Platform Independent
Learning Java is Fun
topics.Hint 2: Each statement should be printed separately.
topics.Code Explanation: The program uses three System.out.println() statements to print three different lines.

topics.title: Topic 2: Structure of a Java Program
topics.Topic Introduction:
A proper structure helps:

Organize code efficiently
Improve readability
Reduce errors
Allow Java Compiler to understand the program
Make maintenance easier

Without proper structure, Java programs will fail to compile.

Basic Java Program Structure
public class Main {

    public static void main(String[] args) {

        System.out.println("Hello Java");

    }

}
Syntax Breakdown
1. Class Declaration
public class Main
Explanation
public → Access modifier
class → Keyword used to create a class
Main → Class name

A class acts as a blueprint that contains code and methods.

2. Main Method
public static void main(String[] args)
Explanation

This is the entry point of every Java application.

When a Java program starts:

JVM searches for main()
Execution begins from main()
3. Statements
System.out.println("Hello Java");
Explanation

A statement performs an action.

This statement prints text on the screen.

4. Curly Braces
{
}
Explanation

Curly braces define blocks of code.

Example:

public class Main {

}

Everything inside belongs to the class.

5. Semicolon
;
Explanation

A semicolon marks the end of a statement.

Example:

System.out.println("Hello");

Without it:

System.out.println("Hello")

Compilation Error.

Visual Structure
Class
 └── main()
      ├── Statement 1
      ├── Statement 2
      └── Statement 3
Real World Usage

Imagine a company building a payroll application.

The project may contain:

Employee.java
Salary.java
Tax.java
Main.java

Each file contains one class.

Java combines these classes to form a complete application.
topics.Definition:
The structure of a Java program refers to the arrangement of classes, methods, and statements that make up a valid Java application.

Every Java application must contain at least one class, and the execution starts from the main() method.
topics.Output:
Student Name: Balu
Course: Java Programming
Phase: 1
Status: Learning
topics.Best Practices:
Use Meaningful Class Names

Good:

Student
Employee
Calculator

Bad:

A
B
X
Maintain Proper Indentation

Good:

public class Main {

    public static void main(String[] args) {

        System.out.println("Hello");

    }
}
Use One Public Class Per File

This improves readability and maintainability.
topics.Key Points:
Every Java program contains at least one class.
Execution starts from main().
Statements end with semicolons.
Curly braces define blocks.
Class name and file name should match.
JVM searches for main() to start execution.
topics.Problem Statement:
Write a Java program that prints the following student information exactly as shown:

Student Name: Balu
Course: Java Programming
Phase: 1
Status: Learning
topics.Output Format: Print four lines exactly as shown.
topics.Constraints:
Use four separate System.out.println() statements.
Follow Java program structure correctly.
topics.Sample Output:
Student Name: Balu
Course: Java Programming
Phase: 1
Status: Learning
topics.Code Explanation:
Step 1

Create a class named Main.

public class Main
Step 2

Create the main method.

public static void main(String[] args)
Step 3

Use four print statements.

System.out.println(...)

to display the required information.

topics.title: Topic 3: Variables and Data Types
topics.Topic Introduction:
Without variables:

System.out.println(20);
System.out.println(20);
System.out.println(20);

If the value changes, we must update it everywhere.

Using variables:

int age = 20;

System.out.println(age);

Now we only change the value once.

Benefits:

Store data efficiently
Reuse values
Improve readability
Reduce code duplication
Understanding Memory

When a variable is created:

int age = 20;

Java stores:

Variable Name     Value
-----------------------
age               20

Inside computer memory.

Variable Declaration Syntax
dataType variableName;

Example:

int age;
Variable Initialization Syntax
dataType variableName = value;

Example:

int age = 20;
Types of Data in Java
Integer

Stores whole numbers.

int age = 20;

Examples:

10
50
100
500
Double

Stores decimal numbers.

double salary = 25000.75;

Examples:

10.5
25.75
99.99
Character

Stores a single character.

char grade = 'A';

Examples:

'A'
'B'
'Z'

Notice single quotes.

Boolean

Stores true or false.

boolean isPassed = true;

Possible values:

true
false
String

Stores text.

String name = "Balu";

Examples:

"Java"
"Programming"
"Student"

Notice double quotes.

Data Type Table
Data Type	Description	Example
int	Whole Numbers	100
double	Decimal Numbers	10.5
char	Single Character	'A'
boolean	True/False	true
String	Text	"Hello"
topics.Definition:
A variable is a named memory location used to store data.

A data type specifies what kind of data a variable can store.
topics.Output:
Name: Balu
Age: 20
Grade: A
Percentage: 85.5
topics.Best Practices:
Use Meaningful Names

Good:

studentAge
employeeSalary
productPrice

Bad:

a
b
x
Follow Camel Case

Good:

studentName
totalMarks

Bad:

student_name
Student_Name
Initialize Variables When Possible

Good:

int age = 20;
topics.Key Points:
Variables store data.
Data types define the kind of data stored.
Java is strongly typed.
Variables must be declared before use.
Strings use double quotes.
Characters use single quotes.
topics.Problem Statement:
Create variables to store the following information and print them:

Name: Balu
Age: 20
Grade: A
Percentage: 85.5
topics.Output Format: Print the values stored in variables.
topics.Sample Output:
Name: Balu
Age: 20
Grade: A
Percentage: 85.5
topics.Hint 2: Use int for age and char for grade.
topics.Code Explanation: The program creates four variables using different data types and prints their values using System.out.println().

topics.title: Topic 4: Operators in Java
topics.Topic Introduction:
Suppose we want to calculate total marks.

Without operators:

System.out.println(90);
System.out.println(80);
System.out.println(70);

This only prints values.

Using operators:

int total = 90 + 80 + 70;

Now Java performs calculations automatically.

Benefits:

Perform calculations
Compare values
Make decisions
Create conditions
Simplify coding
Types of Operators in Java

Java provides several categories of operators:

Arithmetic Operators
Relational Operators
Logical Operators
Assignment Operators
Increment and Decrement Operators
1. Arithmetic Operators

Used for mathematical calculations.

Operator	Meaning
+	Addition
-	Subtraction
*	Multiplication
/	Division
%	Modulus (Remainder)
Addition Example
int a = 10;
int b = 5;

System.out.println(a + b);
topics.Definition:
An operator is a symbol that performs an operation on one or more operands (values or variables).

Example:

int sum = 10 + 20;

Here:

10 and 20 → Operands
+         → Operator
topics.Output:
Sum = 35
Difference = 15
Product = 250
Quotient = 2
Remainder = 5


Quiz 1: Java Fundamentals Quiz
Topics Covered
topics.Common Mistakes:
Mistake 1

Using = instead of ==

Wrong:

if(age = 18)

Correct:

if(age == 18)
Mistake 2

Division by Zero

Wrong:

int result = 10 / 0;

Runtime Error.

Mistake 3

Confusing % with /

10 % 3

Output:

1

Not:

3
Mistake 4

Forgetting Increment Effect

count++;

Actually changes value.
topics.Key Points:
Operators perform actions on values.
Arithmetic operators perform calculations.
Relational operators compare values.
Logical operators combine conditions.
Assignment operators store values.
Increment and decrement modify values by 1.
topics.Problem Statement:
Create a Java program that stores two numbers:

25
10

Display:

Sum
Difference
Product
Quotient
Remainder

using operators.
topics.Constraints:
Use variables.
Use arithmetic operators.
Display all five results.
topics.Code Explanation:
The program creates two integer variables and performs arithmetic operations using Java operators.

Each result is displayed using System.out.println().

topics.title: Topic 5: Taking User Input using Scanner
topics.Topic Introduction:
Without Scanner:

int age = 20;

The value is fixed.

With Scanner:

Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

int age = sc.nextInt();

Now the user decides the value.

Benefits:

Dynamic programs
User interaction
Real-world applications
Flexible inputs
Importing Scanner

Before using Scanner, we must import it.
topics.Definition:
Scanner is a predefined Java class used to read input from the user.

It belongs to the:

java.util

package.
topics.Common Mistakes:
Mistake 1

Forgetting Import Statement

Wrong:

Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

Without:

import java.util.Scanner;

Compiler Error.

Mistake 2

Wrong Method

int age = sc.next();

Wrong because next() returns String.

Correct:

int age = sc.nextInt();
Mistake 3

Entering Text for Integer

Code:

int age = sc.nextInt();

Input:

Balu

Runtime Error.

Mistake 4

Confusing next() and nextLine()

Input:

Balu Krishna

Using:

next()

Only reads:

Balu
topics.Best Practices:
Use Meaningful Object Names

Good:

Scanner input = new Scanner(System.in);
Close Scanner When Finished
sc.close();

(We will discuss this more in advanced phases.)

Use Correct Input Method

Use:

nextInt()

for integers.

Use:

nextDouble()

for decimals.

Use:

nextLine()

for full text.
topics.Key Points:
Scanner is used for user input.
Scanner belongs to java.util package.
Must import Scanner before use.
nextInt() reads integers.
nextDouble() reads decimals.
next() reads one word.
nextLine() reads full lines.
Scanner enables interactive programs.
topics.Problem Statement:
Write a Java program that takes the following inputs from the user:

Name
Age

Then display:

Name: <name>
Age: <age>
topics.Input Format:
First line:

Name

Second line:

Age
topics.Output Format: Display the entered name and age.
topics.Code Explanation:
The program creates a Scanner object and reads:

Name using next()
Age using nextInt()

Then it displays the values using System.out.println().

topics.title: Topic 6: Type Casting in Java
topics.Topic Introduction:
Suppose we have:

int marks = 95;
double percentage;

To store marks inside a double variable:

percentage = marks;

Java converts the value automatically.

Type Casting is useful when:

Performing calculations
Converting user input
Working with different data types
Avoiding compilation errors
Types of Type Casting

Java supports two types:

Widening Casting (Automatic)
Narrowing Casting (Manual)
1. Widening Casting (Automatic Casting)
topics.Definition:
Converting a larger data type into a smaller data type.

This conversion must be done manually.
topics.Common Mistakes:
Mistake 1

Forgetting Casting

Wrong:

double value = 99.99;

int number = value;

Compilation Error.

Correct:

int number = (int) value;
Mistake 2

Expecting Rounded Values

double value = 8.9;

int num = (int) value;

Output:

8

Not:

9

Casting removes decimals.

Mistake 3

Data Loss Ignorance

double salary = 45000.99;

int finalSalary = (int) salary;

Output:

45000

Decimal part is lost.
topics.Key Points:
Type Casting converts one data type into another.
Widening Casting is automatic.
Narrowing Casting is manual.
Narrowing may cause data loss.
Java performs automatic conversions in expressions.
Casting is widely used in calculations and data processing.
topics.Problem Statement:
Create a Java program that:

Stores a decimal value:
95.75
Converts it into an integer using type casting.
Displays both values.
topics.Code Explanation:
The program stores a decimal number in a double variable.

Using:

(int) value

Java converts the double value into an integer.

The decimal part is removed, resulting in:

95

topics.title: Topic 7: Comments in Java
topics.Topic Introduction:
Consider this code:

int a = 5000;
double b = a * 0.05;

A beginner may not understand what it does.

With comments:

// Account balance
int balance = 5000;

// Calculate 5% interest
double interest = balance * 0.05;

The purpose becomes clear.

Benefits:

Improves readability
Makes debugging easier
Helps future maintenance
Assists team collaboration
Types of Comments in Java

Java supports three types of comments:

Single-Line Comments
Multi-Line Comments
Documentation Comments
1. Single-Line Comments
topics.Definition:
Documentation comments are used to generate official Java documentation.

They start with:

/**

and end with:

*/
topics.Common Mistakes:
Mistake 1

Not Closing Multi-Line Comment

Wrong:

/*
This is a comment

Compilation Error.

Correct:

/*
This is a comment
*/
Mistake 2

Writing Too Many Unnecessary Comments

Bad:

// Declare variable
int age = 20;

The code is already obvious.

Mistake 3

Outdated Comments

Bad:

// Store age
double salary = 50000;

Comment does not match code.
topics.Key Points:
Comments are ignored by Java compiler.
Comments improve readability.
Single-line comments use //.
Multi-line comments use /* */.
Documentation comments use /** */.
Good comments explain purpose and intent.
Comments help in maintaining large projects.
topics.Problem Statement:
Create a Java program that:

Uses a single-line comment.
Uses a multi-line comment.
Prints:
Learning Java Comments
topics.Hint 3: Use System.out.println() to display the message.
topics.Code Explanation:
The program demonstrates both:

Single-line comments (//)
Multi-line comments (/* */)

Comments are ignored by Java during execution.

Only the print statement produces output.

topics.title: Topic 8: Escape Sequences in Java
topics.Topic Introduction:
Without escape sequences:

System.out.println("Hello Java");

Output:

Hello Java

Everything appears on one line.

With escape sequences:

System.out.println("Hello\nJava");

Output:

Hello
Java

Benefits:

Better formatting
Cleaner output
Professional-looking programs
Easy report generation
Common Escape Sequences
Escape Sequence	Meaning
\n	New Line
\t	Tab Space
"	Double Quote
'	Single Quote
\	Backslash
1. New Line Escape Sequence (\n)
topics.Output:
Student Report

Name:    Balu
Age:     20
Course:  Java

"Learning Java is Fun"


Quiz 2: Scanner, Type Casting, Comments & Escape Sequences
Topics Covered
topics.Common Mistakes:
Mistake 1

Forgetting Backslash

Wrong:

System.out.println("Hello n Java");

Correct:

System.out.println("Hello\nJava");
Mistake 2

Using Single Backslash for Paths

Wrong:

"C:\Java"

Correct:

"C:\\Java"
Mistake 3

Printing Quotes Directly

Wrong:

"Java is "Easy""

Correct:

"Java is \"Easy\""
topics.Best Practices:
Use \n for Readable Output

Good:

System.out.println("Name\nAge");
Use \t for Tables

Good:

System.out.println("Product\tPrice");
Escape File Paths Properly

Good:

"C:\\Users\\Balu"
Combine Escape Sequences When Needed

Example:

System.out.println("Name:\tBalu\nAge:\t20");
topics.Key Points:
Escape sequences begin with \.
\n creates a new line.
\t creates tab spacing.
\" prints double quotes.
\' prints single quotes.
\\ prints a backslash.
Escape sequences improve output formatting.
topics.Problem Statement:
Write a Java program that displays the following formatted output using escape sequences:

Student Report

Name:    Balu
Age:     20
Course:  Java

"Learning Java is Fun"
topics.Output Format: Display the formatted report exactly as shown.
topics.Sample Output:
Student Report

Name:    Balu
Age:     20
Course:  Java

"Learning Java is Fun"
topics.Code Explanation:
The program uses:

\n to move to a new line
\t to create spacing
\" to print quotation marks

These escape sequences help create a properly formatted report.

topics.title: Topic 5: Taking User Input using Scanner
Topic 1: Introduction to Decision Making
topics.Topic Introduction:
Without decision making:

System.out.println("Eligible for Voting");

This always prints the message.

Even if the person's age is 10.

With decision making:

if(age >= 18)
{
    System.out.println("Eligible");
}

The message appears only when the condition is true.

Understanding Conditions

A condition is an expression that evaluates to:

true

or

false

Example:

10 > 5

Result:

true

Example:

3 > 10

Result:

false
Relational Operators Used in Conditions
Operator	Meaning
==	Equal To
!=	Not Equal To
>	Greater Than
<	Less Than
>=	Greater Than Or Equal
<=	Less Than Or Equal
Example
int age = 20;

System.out.println(age >= 18);

Output:

true
Flow of Decision Making
Condition

   ↓

True ?

  / \
Yes  No

↓      ↓

Execute
Code

Skip
Code
Real World Examples
ATM
Balance > Withdrawal Amount
Login System
Username and Password Correct
Online Shopping
Product Available
College Admission
Marks >= Cutoff
topics.Definition: Decision Making is the process of executing different blocks of code depending on whether a condition is true or false.
topics.Common Mistakes:
Mistake 1

Using Assignment Operator Instead of Comparison Operator

Wrong:

age = 18

Correct:

age == 18
Mistake 2

Confusing > and >=

Example:

age >= 18

includes 18.

age > 18

does not include 18.

Mistake 3

Expecting Non-Boolean Results

Condition:

10 > 5

returns:

true

not:

10
topics.Best Practices:
Use Meaningful Variable Names

Good:

studentMarks
userAge
accountBalance
Write Readable Conditions

Good:

age >= 18

Bad:

18 <= age
Keep Conditions Simple

Good:

marks >= 35
topics.Key Points:
Decision making allows programs to choose actions.
Conditions return true or false.
Relational operators help create conditions.
Real-world applications depend heavily on decision making.
Decision making is the foundation of if, if-else, switch, and loops.
topics.Problem Statement:
Create a Java program that stores:

Age = 20

Display the result of:

Age >= 18

using a condition.
topics.Hint 1: Create an integer variable named age.
topics.Code Explanation:
The variable age stores 20.

The condition:

age >= 18

evaluates to:

true

because 20 is greater than 18.

topics.title: Topic 2: if Statement
topics.Definition: An if statement is a decision-making statement that executes a block of code only if a specified condition evaluates to true.
topics.Syntax:
if(condition)
{
    // code to execute
}
Syntax Breakdown
if

A Java keyword used for decision making.

condition

A boolean expression that returns:

true

or

false
Curly Braces
{
}

Contain the code to execute when the condition is true.

Flow of Execution
Condition

   ↓

True ?

 /    \

Yes    No

↓       ↓

Execute  Skip
Code     Code
How if Statement Works

Step 1:

Java checks the condition.

Step 2:

If condition is true:

Execute code block

Step 3:

If condition is false:

Skip code block

Step 4:

Continue executing the remaining program.

Real World Examples
Voting System
If age >= 18
    Allow Voting
ATM
If PIN Correct
    Open Account Menu
Login System
If Password Correct
    Login Successful
Online Shopping
If Product Available
    Place Order
topics.Output:
Pass

Phase 3 - Quiz 1
Topics Covered

✅ Topic 1: Introduction to Loops
✅ Topic 2: while Loop
✅ Topic 3: do-while Loop
✅ Topic 4: for Loop

Question 1

What is the primary purpose of a loop?

A. To take input

B. To store data

C. To repeat a block of code

D. To create classes

✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation

Loops are used to execute a block of code repeatedly until a condition becomes false.

Question 2

Which of the following are the three common components of a loop?

A. Scanner, Variable, Method

B. Initialization, Condition, Update

C. Class, Object, Method

D. Input, Output, Process

✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation

Every loop generally contains:

Initialization
Condition
Update
Question 3

Which loop checks the condition before execution?

A. do-while

B. switch

C. if

D. while

✅ Correct Answer: D

Explanation

The while loop checks its condition before executing the loop body.

Question 4

Which loop guarantees at least one execution?

A. while

B. for

C. do-while

D. if

✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation

The do-while loop executes first and checks the condition afterward.

Question 5

Which loop is most suitable when the number of iterations is known?

A. while

B. do-while

C. for

D. switch

✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation

The for loop is designed for situations where the number of repetitions is known beforehand.

Question 6

Which loop is called an exit-controlled loop?

A. while

B. do-while

C. for

D. switch

✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation

The condition in a do-while loop is checked after execution.

Question 7 (Output Prediction)

What will be the output?

int i = 1;

while(i <= 3)
{
    System.out.println(i);
    i++;
}

A.

1
2
3

B.

1
2
3
4

C.

0
1
2
3

D. Infinite Loop

✅ Correct Answer: A

Explanation

The loop runs while:

i <= 3

and prints:

1
2
3
Question 8 (Output Prediction)

What will be the output?

int i = 10;

do
{
    System.out.println(i);
}
while(i <= 5);

A. No Output

B.

10

C.

5

D. Infinite Loop

✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation

A do-while loop executes once before checking the condition.

Question 9 (Error Finding)

Find the error:

int i = 1;

while(i <= 5)
{
    System.out.println(i);
}

A. Missing Scanner

B. Missing condition

C. Missing update

D. Missing semicolon

✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation

Without:

i++;

the value of i never changes, causing an infinite loop.

Question 10 (Scenario Based)

A school system needs to print roll numbers from:

1 to 100

Which loop is most suitable?

A. if

B. switch

C. for

D. Scanner

✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation

The number of iterations is known (100), making a for loop the best choice.
topics.Key Points:
if statement executes code only when condition is true.
Condition must return true or false.
If condition is false, code block is skipped.
Curly braces improve readability.
if statement is the foundation of decision making in Java.
topics.Problem Statement:
Create a Java program that stores:

Marks = 80

If marks are greater than or equal to:

35

display:

Pass
topics.Code Explanation:
The program creates a variable:

int marks = 80;

The condition:

marks >= 35

returns:

true

because 80 is greater than 35.

Therefore the code inside the if block executes and prints:

Pass

topics.title: Topic 5: Nested Loops
topics.Topic Introduction:
Suppose a classroom has:

3 Rows
4 Students Per Row

To display every student position:

Row 1 → Student 1,2,3,4
Row 2 → Student 1,2,3,4
Row 3 → Student 1,2,3,4

We need:

One loop for rows
One loop for students

This is exactly what nested loops provide.
topics.Definition:
A Nested Loop is a loop placed inside another loop.

The outer loop controls how many times the inner loop runs.
topics.Best Practices:
Use Meaningful Names

Good:

row
col

Instead of:

i
j

for patterns.

Format Output Carefully

Use:

System.out.print()

inside.

Use:

System.out.println()

after inner loop.

Understand Execution Count

Formula:

Outer Loop × Inner Loop
topics.Key Points:
Nested Loop = Loop inside another loop.
Outer loop controls repetitions.
Inner loop executes completely every outer iteration.
Used heavily in patterns, matrices, tables, and games.
Total executions = Outer Loop × Inner Loop.
topics.Problem Statement:
Write a Java program that prints the following pattern using nested loops:

*
* *
* * *
* * * *
* * * * *
topics.Hint 1: Use an outer loop for rows.
topics.Hint 2: Use an inner loop for columns.
topics.Hint 3: Inner loop should run up to the current row number.
topics.Code Explanation:
The outer loop controls:

Rows

from:

1 to 5

The inner loop controls:

Columns

and runs up to the current row number.

Therefore:

Row 1 → 1 star
Row 2 → 2 stars
Row 3 → 3 stars
Row 4 → 4 stars
Row 5 → 5 stars

topics.title: Topic 6: break Statement
topics.Definition:
The break statement is used to immediately terminate a loop or switch statement.

When Java encounters a break statement, control exits the loop and moves to the next statement after the loop.
topics.Syntax:
break;
Flow of Execution
Loop Starts

     ↓

Execute

     ↓

break Reached?

   /      \

 Yes      No

 ↓          ↓

Exit      Continue
Loop      Loop
topics.Common Mistakes:
Mistake 1

Thinking break Stops Entire Program

Wrong assumption:

break stops program

Correct:

break stops only the current loop or switch
Mistake 2

Using break Outside Loop

Wrong:

break;

outside loop/switch.

Compilation Error.

Mistake 3

Confusing break and continue

break

means:

Stop Loop
continue

means:

Skip Current Iteration
Mistake 4

Expecting break to Exit All Nested Loops

Default behavior:

Only exits nearest loop
topics.Key Points:
break immediately exits a loop or switch.
Used for early termination.
Saves unnecessary processing.
Commonly used in search, login, and menu systems.
In nested loops, break exits only the nearest loop.
topics.Problem Statement:
Write a Java program that prints numbers from:

1 to 10

but stops when:

5

is reached.

Output should be:

1
2
3
4
topics.Code Explanation:
The loop starts from:

i = 1

When:

i == 5

becomes true,

Java executes:

break;

and exits the loop immediately.

Therefore only:

1
2
3
4

are printed.

topics.title: Topic 7: continue Statement
topics.Definition:
The continue statement is used to skip the current iteration of a loop and immediately move to the next iteration.

Unlike break, it does not terminate the loop.
topics.Syntax:
continue;
Flow of Execution
Loop Starts

      ↓

Condition

      ↓

continue Reached?

   /        \

 Yes        No

 ↓            ↓

Skip      Execute
Iteration  Remaining Code

 ↓

Next Iteration
Difference Between break and continue
Feature	break	continue
Stops Loop	Yes	No
Skips Iteration	No	Yes
Continues Loop	No	Yes
Common Usage	Exit Early	Skip Specific Values
topics.Common Mistakes:
Mistake 1

Confusing break and continue

break

means:

Stop Loop
continue

means:

Skip Current Iteration
Mistake 2

Using continue Before Update in while Loop

Can cause:

Infinite Loop
Mistake 3

Expecting continue to Stop Loop

Wrong assumption:

continue ends loop

Correct:

continue skips only one iteration
Mistake 4

Using continue Outside Loop

Wrong:

continue;

outside a loop.

Compilation Error.
topics.Key Points:
continue skips the current iteration.
Loop execution continues normally afterward.
Does not terminate the loop.
Commonly used for filtering unwanted values.
Very useful in data processing and validation.
topics.Problem Statement:
Write a Java program that prints numbers from:

1 to 10

but skips:

5

using the continue statement.
topics.Code Explanation:
The loop prints numbers from:

1 to 10

When:

i == 5

Java executes:

continue;

which skips the print statement for that iteration.

Therefore:

5

is not printed.

topics.title: Topic 8: Enhanced for Loop (for-each Loop)
topics.Definition:
An Enhanced for Loop (also called a for-each Loop) is a simplified loop used to traverse arrays and collections without using an index variable.

It automatically accesses each element one by one.
topics.Output:
5
10
15
20
25
Sum = 75

Phase 3 - Quiz 2
Topics Covered

✅ Topic 5: Nested Loops
✅ Topic 6: break Statement
✅ Topic 7: continue Statement
✅ Topic 8: Enhanced for Loop

Question 1

What is a Nested Loop?

A. A loop that never stops

B. A loop inside another loop

C. A loop with break

D. A loop with continue

✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation

A Nested Loop is a loop placed inside another loop.

Question 2

If an outer loop runs 4 times and an inner loop runs 3 times, how many total executions occur?

A. 7

B. 12

C. 4

D. 3

✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation

Formula:

Outer Loop × Inner Loop
4 × 3 = 12
Question 3

What does the break statement do?

A. Skips current iteration

B. Restarts loop

C. Exits current loop or switch

D. Creates infinite loop

✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation

break immediately terminates the current loop or switch statement.

Question 4

What does continue do?

A. Ends program

B. Ends loop

C. Skips current iteration and continues

D. Restarts program

✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation

continue skips only the current iteration.

Question 5

Which loop is also called a for-each loop?

A. while

B. do-while

C. Enhanced for Loop

D. Nested Loop

✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation

Enhanced for Loop is commonly called the for-each Loop.

Question 6

Which of the following is an advantage of Enhanced for Loop?

A. Direct index access

B. Less code and better readability

C. Reverse traversal

D. Random access

✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation

Enhanced for Loop reduces code and improves readability.

Question 7 (Output Prediction)

What will be the output?

for(int i = 1; i <= 5; i++)
{
    if(i == 3)
    {
        break;
    }

    System.out.println(i);
}

A.

1
2

B.

1
2
3

C.

1
2
3
4
5

D. No Output

✅ Correct Answer: A

Explanation

When:

i == 3

break executes before printing.

Question 8 (Output Prediction)

What will be the output?

for(int i = 1; i <= 5; i++)
{
    if(i == 3)
    {
        continue;
    }

    System.out.println(i);
}

A.

1
2
3
4
5

B.

1
2
4
5

C.

3

D. No Output

✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation

The value:

3

is skipped.

Question 9 (Error Finding)

Find the error:

String[] names =
{
    "A",
    "B"
};

for(int name : names)
{
    System.out.println(name);
}

A. Missing loop

B. Missing braces

C. Wrong data type

D. Missing semicolon

✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation

Array elements are:

String

So loop variable must also be:

String

Correct:

for(String name : names)
Question 10 (Scenario Based)

A game map contains:

Rows
Columns

Which concept is most suitable?

A. switch

B. if

C. Nested Loops

D. Scanner

✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation

Rows and columns naturally require Nested Loops.

Phase 3 Final Quiz: Loops & Iteration
Topics Covered

✅ Introduction to Loops
✅ while Loop
✅ do-while Loop
✅ for Loop
✅ Nested Loops
✅ break Statement
✅ continue Statement
✅ Enhanced for Loop

Question 1

What is the main purpose of loops?

A. Create classes

B. Store values

C. Repeat code

D. Take input

✅ Correct Answer: C

Question 2

Which are the three common components of a loop?

A. Class, Object, Method

B. Initialization, Condition, Update

C. Input, Output, Process

D. Array, Loop, Method

✅ Correct Answer: B

Question 3

Which loop checks the condition before execution?

A. do-while

B. while

C. switch

D. if

✅ Correct Answer: B

Question 4

Which loop guarantees at least one execution?

A. while

B. for

C. do-while

D. switch

✅ Correct Answer: C

Question 5

Which loop is best when the number of iterations is known?

A. while

B. do-while

C. for

D. switch

✅ Correct Answer: C

Question 6

What is a Nested Loop?

A. Loop outside program

B. Infinite loop

C. Loop inside another loop

D. Loop with break

✅ Correct Answer: C

Question 7

What does break do?

A. Skip current iteration

B. Exit current loop or switch

C. Restart loop

D. End program

✅ Correct Answer: B

Question 8

What does continue do?

A. End loop

B. End program

C. Skip current iteration

D. Restart program

✅ Correct Answer: C

Question 9

Enhanced for Loop is also called:

A. while loop

B. nested loop

C. for-each loop

D. infinite loop

✅ Correct Answer: C

Question 10

Which loop is an exit-controlled loop?

A. for

B. while

C. do-while

D. nested loop

✅ Correct Answer: C

Question 11 (Output Prediction)
int i = 1;

while(i <= 3)
{
    System.out.println(i);
    i++;
}

Output?

A.

1
2
3

B.

1
2
3
4

C.

0
1
2
3

D. Infinite Loop

✅ Correct Answer: A

Question 12 (Output Prediction)
int i = 10;

do
{
    System.out.println(i);
}
while(i <= 5);

Output?

A. No Output

B.

10

C.

5

D. Infinite Loop

✅ Correct Answer: B

Question 13 (Output Prediction)
for(int i = 1; i <= 5; i++)
{
    if(i == 3)
    {
        break;
    }

    System.out.println(i);
}

Output?

A.

1
2

B.

1
2
3

C.

1
2
3
4
5

D. No Output

✅ Correct Answer: A

Question 14 (Output Prediction)
for(int i = 1; i <= 5; i++)
{
    if(i == 3)
    {
        continue;
    }

    System.out.println(i);
}

Output?

A.

1
2
4
5

B.

1
2
3
4
5

C.

3

D. No Output

✅ Correct Answer: A

Question 15 (Output Prediction)
for(int row = 1; row <= 2; row++)
{
    for(int col = 1; col <= 2; col++)
    {
        System.out.print("* ");
    }

    System.out.println();
}

Output?

A.

*
*

B.

* *
* *

C.

* * *
* * *

D.

**
**

✅ Correct Answer: B

Question 16 (Error Finding)
int i = 1;

while(i <= 5)
{
    System.out.println(i);
}

What is missing?

A. Scanner

B. Condition

C. Update

D. break

✅ Correct Answer: C

Question 17 (Error Finding)
String[] names =
{
    "A",
    "B"
};

for(int name : names)
{
}

What is wrong?

A. Missing loop

B. Missing array

C. Wrong data type

D. Missing semicolon

✅ Correct Answer: C

Question 18 (Scenario Based)

A multiplication table from:

1 to 10

is best implemented using:

A. switch

B. for loop

C. if statement

D. Scanner

✅ Correct Answer: B

Question 19 (Scenario Based)

A chess board contains:

8 Rows
8 Columns

Which concept is most suitable?

A. if

B. switch

C. Nested Loops

D. continue

✅ Correct Answer: C

Question 20 (Scenario Based)

A search operation should stop immediately when an item is found.

Which statement should be used?

A. continue

B. if

C. switch

D. break

✅ Correct Answer: D
topics.Key Points:
Enhanced for Loop is also called for-each Loop.
Used for arrays and collections.
Automatically accesses elements one by one.
Easier to read than traditional for loops.
Cannot directly access indexes.
Best for reading data.
topics.Problem Statement:
Create an array:

{5, 10, 15, 20, 25}

Using an Enhanced for Loop:

Display every element.
Calculate and display the total sum.
topics.Code Explanation:
The enhanced for loop automatically accesses:

5
10
15
20
25

one by one.

For every element:

sum += num;

adds it to the total.

Final sum:

75

topics.title: Topic 1: Introduction to Arrays
topics.Topic Introduction:
Without arrays:

int student1 = 80;
int student2 = 85;
int student3 = 90;
int student4 = 75;
int student5 = 95;

Problems:

Too many variables
Difficult maintenance
Hard to process using loops

With arrays:

int[] students = {80, 85, 90, 75, 95};

Advantages:

Single variable
Easy processing
Works perfectly with loops
Real World Examples
Student Marks
85
90
78
95
88

Stored in:

int[] marks
Product Prices
100
250
300
450

Stored in:

double[] prices
Employee Names
Rahul
Anjali
Balu

Stored in:

String[] names
Monthly Sales
Jan
Feb
Mar
...

Stored in arrays.

Characteristics of Arrays
Same Data Type

Valid:

int[] numbers =
{
    10,
    20,
    30
};

Invalid:

int[] numbers =
{
    10,
    "Java",
    30
};

Different data types are not allowed.

Fixed Size

Once created:

int[] arr = new int[5];

Size cannot change.

Indexed

Every element has an index.

Example:

int[] marks =
{
    85,
    90,
    78,
    95,
    88
};
Index	Value
0	85
1	90
2	78
3	95
4	88
Understanding Indexes

Arrays start from:

0

Not:

1

Example:

int[] numbers =
{
    10,
    20,
    30
};
Index	Value
0	10
1	20
2	30
Visual Representation
numbers

 ┌────┬────┬────┐
 │10  │20  │30  │
 └────┴────┴────┘

   0    1    2
topics.Definition: An Array is a collection of elements of the same data type stored in contiguous memory locations and accessed using indexes.
topics.Common Mistakes:
Mistake 1

Thinking Index Starts From 1

Wrong:

marks[1]

for first element.

Correct:

marks[0]
Mistake 2

Mixing Data Types

Wrong:

int[] arr =
{
    10,
    "Java"
};
Mistake 3

Confusing Array Name and Elements

Wrong:

marks

means entire array.

marks[0]

means one element.

Mistake 4

Ignoring Fixed Size

Arrays cannot automatically grow.
topics.Best Practices:
Use Meaningful Names

Good:

studentMarks
employeeNames
productPrices
Store Related Data Together

Good:

int[] marks
Remember Index Starts at 0

Always verify indexes carefully.
topics.Key Points:
Arrays store multiple values in one variable.
All elements must be the same data type.
Arrays use indexes.
Indexes start from 0.
Arrays have fixed size.
Arrays work extremely well with loops.
topics.Problem Statement:
A school wants to store marks of 5 students:

85
90
78
95
88

Create an integer array to store these values.

Display:

Array Created Successfully
topics.Hint 2: Store all 5 marks inside the array.
topics.Code Explanation:
The array:

int[] marks

stores:

85
90
78
95
88

inside a single variable.

The program then displays a success message.

topics.title: Topic 2: Creating and Initializing Arrays
topics.Topic Introduction:
In the previous topic, we learned what an array is and why arrays are important.

Now we will learn how to actually create arrays in Java.

Creating an array generally happens in three steps:

Declaration
Creation
Initialization

Understanding these steps is very important because every Java developer uses arrays regularly.
topics.Common Mistakes:
Mistake 1

Using Invalid Index

Wrong:

int[] arr = new int[3];

arr[3] = 50;

Valid indexes:

0
1
2
Mistake 2

Forgetting new

Wrong:

int[] arr;

arr[0] = 10;

Memory not allocated.

Mistake 3

Wrong Size Assumption

new int[5]

means:

Indexes:
0 to 4

not:

1 to 5
Mistake 4

Mixing Data Types

Wrong:

int[] arr =
{
    10,
    "Java"
};

Compilation Error.
topics.Best Practices:
Use Meaningful Names

Good:

studentMarks
productPrices
employeeNames
Initialize Arrays Properly

Avoid partially initialized arrays when possible.

Use Direct Initialization for Known Values

Good:

int[] arr =
{
    10,
    20,
    30
};
Remember Index Starts at 0

Always verify array size and indexes.
topics.Key Points:
Arrays are created using declaration, creation, and initialization.
Memory is allocated using:
new
Arrays can be initialized directly.
Arrays have fixed size.
Default values are automatically assigned.
Indexes start from 0.
topics.Problem Statement:
Create an integer array that stores:

10
20
30
40
50

using direct initialization.

Display:

Array Initialized Successfully
topics.Code Explanation:
The array:

int[] numbers

stores:

10
20
30
40
50

using direct initialization.

The program then displays a confirmation message.

topics.title: Topic 3: Accessing Array Elements
topics.Topic Introduction:
In the previous topic, we learned how to:

Declare Arrays
Create Arrays
Initialize Arrays

Creating an array is useful, but the real power of arrays comes from accessing and using the stored values.

Example:

int[] marks =
{
    85,
    90,
    78,
    95,
    88
};

Now the question is:

How do we retrieve 85?
How do we retrieve 90?
How do we retrieve 95?

The answer is:

Using Indexes

Every element inside an array has an index position.
topics.Definition:
Accessing Array Elements means retrieving or reading values stored inside an array using their index positions.

Understanding Indexes

Arrays always start indexing from:

0

not:

1

Example:

int[] marks =
{
    85,
    90,
    78,
    95,
    88
};
Index	Value
0	85
1	90
2	78
3	95
4	88
Visual Representation
marks

 ┌────┬────┬────┬────┬────┐
 │85  │90  │78  │95  │88  │
 └────┴────┴────┴────┴────┘

   0    1    2    3    4
topics.Syntax:
arrayName[index]

Example:

marks[0]

returns:

85
Accessing Individual Elements

Example:

int[] marks =
{
    85,
    90,
    78,
    95,
    88
};
First Element
marks[0]

Output:

85
Second Element
marks[1]

Output:

90
Third Element
marks[2]

Output:

78
Fourth Element
marks[3]

Output:

95
Fifth Element
marks[4]

Output:

88
topics.Common Mistakes:
Mistake 1

Thinking Arrays Start From 1

Wrong:

marks[1]

for first element.

Correct:

marks[0]
Mistake 2

Using Invalid Index

Wrong:

numbers[10]

when array size is:

5
Mistake 3

Using Length as Index

Wrong:

numbers[numbers.length]

For array size 5:

length = 5

Valid indexes:

0
1
2
3
4

Correct:

numbers[numbers.length - 1]
Mistake 4

Negative Index

Wrong:

numbers[-1]

Not allowed.
topics.Best Practices:
Verify Index Range

Always ensure:

0 ≤ index < length
Use length for Dynamic Access

Good:

numbers[numbers.length - 1]
Use Meaningful Array Names

Good:

studentMarks
employeeNames
productPrices
topics.Key Points:
Array elements are accessed using indexes.
Indexing starts from 0.
Syntax:
arrayName[index]
Invalid indexes cause:
ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
Array size is obtained using:
arrayName.length
topics.Problem Statement:
Create an array:

10
20
30
40
50

Display:

First element
Third element
Last element

Expected Output:

10
30
50
topics.Hint 3:
Use:

arrayName.length - 1

for the last element.
topics.Code Explanation:
The first element is:

numbers[0]

The third element is:

numbers[2]

The last element is:

numbers[numbers.length - 1]

which evaluates to:

numbers[4]

topics.title: Topic 4: Updating Array Elements
topics.Topic Introduction:
In the previous topic, we learned how to access array elements using indexes.

Example:

int[] marks =
{
    85,
    90,
    78,
    95,
    88
};

System.out.println(marks[2]);

Output:

78

Reading values is useful.

But in real-world applications, data often changes.

Examples:

Student marks get corrected
Product prices change
Employee salaries increase
Game scores are updated

To handle these situations, Java allows us to update array elements.
topics.Definition: Updating Array Elements means replacing an existing value stored in an array with a new value using its index.
topics.Syntax:
arrayName[index] = newValue;

Example:

marks[2] = 80;
Explanation

Before:

Index  Value

0      85
1      90
2      78
3      95
4      88

Statement:

marks[2] = 80;

After:

Index  Value

0      85
1      90
2      80
3      95
4      88

The old value:

78

is replaced by:

80
Visual Representation

Before:

 ┌────┬────┬────┬────┬────┐
 │85  │90  │78  │95  │88  │
 └────┴────┴────┴────┴────┘

   0    1    2    3    4

Update:

marks[2] = 80;

After:

 ┌────┬────┬────┬────┬────┐
 │85  │90  │80  │95  │88  │
 └────┴────┴────┴────┴────┘

   0    1    2    3    4
topics.Output:
10
20
100
40
50

Phase 4 - Quiz 1
Topics Covered

✅ Topic 1: Introduction to Arrays
✅ Topic 2: Creating and Initializing Arrays
✅ Topic 3: Accessing Array Elements
✅ Topic 4: Updating Array Elements

Question 1

What is an Array?

A. A single variable storing one value

B. A collection of elements of the same data type

C. A loop

D. A method

✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation

Arrays store multiple values of the same data type in a single variable.

Question 2

What is the starting index of an array?

A. 1

B. -1

C. 0

D. Depends on size

✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation

Java arrays always start indexing from:

0
Question 3

Which keyword allocates memory for an array?

A. create

B. make

C. array

D. new

✅ Correct Answer: D

Explanation

Example:

int[] numbers = new int[5];
Question 4

Which property gives the size of an array?

A. size

B. count

C. length

D. total

✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation

Example:

numbers.length
Question 5

Which statement correctly accesses the first element?

A.

numbers[1]

B.

numbers[0]

C.

numbers[-1]

D.

numbers[length]

✅ Correct Answer: B

Question 6

How do you update an array element?

A.

numbers.add(10);

B.

numbers[2] = 10;

C.

update(numbers);

D.

numbers.create(10);

✅ Correct Answer: B

Question 7 (Output Prediction)

What will be the output?

int[] numbers =
{
    10,
    20,
    30
};

System.out.println(numbers[1]);

A.

10

B.

20

C.

30

D. Error

✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation

Index:

0 → 10
1 → 20
2 → 30
Question 8 (Output Prediction)

What will be the output?

int[] numbers =
{
    10,
    20,
    30
};

numbers[2] = 100;

System.out.println(numbers[2]);

A.

30

B.

20

C.

100

D. Error

✅ Correct Answer: C

Question 9 (Error Finding)

Find the error:

int[] numbers =
{
    10,
    20,
    30
};

System.out.println(numbers[3]);

A. Missing array

B. Missing semicolon

C. Invalid index

D. Wrong data type

✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation

Valid indexes:

0
1
2

Index:

numbers[3]

does not exist.

Question 10 (Scenario Based)

A school wants to store marks of 500 students.

Which is the best option?

A. 500 separate variables

B. switch statement

C. Array

D. Nested Loop

✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation

Arrays are designed to store multiple values of the same type efficiently.
topics.Common Mistakes:
Mistake 1

Using Invalid Index

Wrong:

numbers[10] = 50;

when array size is:

5

Results in:

ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
Mistake 2

Using Wrong Data Type

Wrong:

int[] marks =
{
    80,
    90
};

marks[0] = "Java";

Compilation Error.

Mistake 3

Thinking Update Creates New Element

Wrong assumption:

Update adds a new element

Correct:

Update replaces existing value
Mistake 4

Negative Index

Wrong:

numbers[-1] = 50;

Not allowed.
topics.Best Practices:
Verify Index Before Updating

Good:

0 <= index < array.length
Use Meaningful Names

Good:

studentMarks
productPrices
employeeNames
Update Only When Necessary

Avoid unnecessary changes.

Validate User Input

Before updating:

numbers[index]

ensure the index is valid.
topics.Key Points:
Array elements can be modified using indexes.
Syntax:
arrayName[index] = newValue;
Updating replaces an existing value.
Invalid indexes cause:
ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
Arrays remain the same size after updates.
topics.Problem Statement:
Create an array:

10
20
30
40
50

Update:

30 → 100

Then display all values.

Expected Output:

10
20
100
40
50
topics.Hint 3: Use a loop to print all elements.
topics.Code Explanation:
Initially:

10
20
30
40
50

Statement:

numbers[2] = 100;

replaces:

30

with:

100

The enhanced for loop prints all updated values.

topics.title: Topic 5: Array Traversal
topics.Definition: Array Traversal is the process of visiting and accessing every element of an array one by one.
topics.Common Mistakes:
Mistake 1

Using:

i <= numbers.length

Wrong:

for(int i = 0; i <= numbers.length; i++)

Causes:

ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException

Correct:

i < numbers.length
Mistake 2

Starting From Index 1

Wrong:

for(int i = 1; i < numbers.length; i++)

Skips first element.

Mistake 3

Forgetting Increment

Wrong:

while(i < numbers.length)
{
}

Infinite loop.

Mistake 4

Using Enhanced for Loop When Index Needed

Enhanced for loop provides values only.

Not indexes.
topics.Key Points:
Traversal means visiting every array element.
Most common loop:
for(int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++)
Enhanced for loop is simpler.
Reverse traversal starts from:
arr.length - 1
Always use:
i < arr.length

not:

i <= arr.length
topics.Problem Statement:
Create an array:

10
20
30
40
50

Display all elements using array traversal.

Expected Output:

10
20
30
40
50
topics.Code Explanation:
The loop starts from:

i = 0

and continues until:

i < numbers.length

Each iteration prints:

numbers[i]

which accesses the current array element.

topics.title: Topic 6: Array Input from User
topics.Topic Introduction:
Hardcoded values:

int[] numbers =
{
    10,
    20,
    30
};

Always stores the same values.

User Input:

15
25
35

The program becomes dynamic and can work with different inputs every time.

Scanner and Arrays

To take input:

import java.util.Scanner;

Create Scanner object:

Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

Read values:

sc.nextInt();

Store them into array elements.

Basic Process
Step 1

Create array.

int[] numbers = new int[5];
Step 2

Use loop.

for(int i = 0; i < numbers.length; i++)
Step 3

Take input.

numbers[i] = sc.nextInt();
topics.Definition: Array Input is the process of reading values from the user and storing them into array elements.
topics.Common Mistakes:
Mistake 1

Using Wrong Loop Condition

Wrong:

for(int i = 0; i <= arr.length; i++)

Causes:

ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException

Correct:

i < arr.length
Mistake 2

Not Creating Array First

Wrong:

arr[i] = sc.nextInt();

before array creation.

Mistake 3

Wrong Input Method

For String arrays:

Wrong:

nextInt()

Correct:

next()
Mistake 4

Forgetting Import

Wrong:

Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

without:

import java.util.Scanner;
topics.Best Practices:
Always Use arr.length

Good:

for(int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++)
Validate User Input

Ensure values are sensible.

Use Meaningful Names

Good:

studentMarks
productPrices
employeeNames
Use Enhanced for Loop for Display

Good:

for(int num : arr)
topics.Key Points:
Array input stores user-provided values into array elements.
Scanner is commonly used.
Input loop:
arr[i] = sc.nextInt();
Dynamic array size is possible.
Always use:
i < arr.length

for traversal.
topics.Problem Statement:
Create an integer array of size:

5

Take 5 numbers from the user.

Display all entered numbers.

topics.title: Topic 7: Finding Sum, Average, Minimum and Maximum in Arrays
topics.Topic Introduction:
In previous topics, we learned how to:

Create Arrays
Access Elements
Update Elements
Traverse Arrays
Take Input into Arrays

Now we will perform actual calculations on array data.

Suppose:

int[] marks =
{
    85,
    90,
    78,
    95,
    88
};

Questions we might ask:

What is the total of all marks?
What is the average mark?
What is the highest mark?
What is the lowest mark?

These operations are extremely common in real-world applications.

Examples:

Student Result Systems
Banking Applications
Sales Reports
Analytics Dashboards
AI Data Processing
Part 1: Finding Sum of Array Elements
topics.Definition:
The minimum value is the smallest element in the array.

Example

Array:

85
90
78
95
88

Minimum:

78
Logic
Assume first element is minimum.
Compare every element.
Replace minimum when a smaller value is found.
topics.Best Practices:
Use Enhanced for Loop

Good:

for(int num : arr)
Initialize Max and Min with First Element

Good:

int max = arr[0];
int min = arr[0];
Use Double for Average

Good:

double avg
Combine Calculations in One Traversal

Efficient:

sum
max
min

inside a single loop.
topics.Key Points:
Sum = Total of all elements.
Average = Sum ÷ Number of Elements.
Maximum = Largest value.
Minimum = Smallest value.
Enhanced for loop simplifies calculations.
Use first element for max and min initialization.
topics.Problem Statement:
Create an array:

10
20
30
40
50

Find and display:

Sum
Average
Maximum
Minimum

Expected Output:

Sum = 150
Average = 30.0
Maximum = 50
Minimum = 10
topics.Code Explanation:
The loop visits every element.

During traversal:

sum += num;

calculates total.

if(num > max)

finds the largest value.

if(num < min)

finds the smallest value.

Finally:

(double) sum / numbers.length

calculates the average.

topics.title: Topic 8: Searching Elements (Linear Search)
topics.Topic Introduction:
Imagine you have an array:

int[] numbers =
{
    10,
    20,
    30,
    40,
    50
};

Now someone asks:

Is 30 present in the array?

or

At which index is 40 stored?

To answer these questions, we need Searching.

Searching is one of the most important operations in programming.

Real-world examples:

Search a student by roll number.
Search a product by ID.
Search a contact by phone number.
Search a user by username.
topics.Definition:
Searching is the process of finding whether a specific element exists in a collection of data.

What is Linear Search?

Linear Search is the simplest searching technique.

It checks elements one by one from the beginning until:

Element Found

or

End of Array Reached
How Linear Search Works

Array:

10 20 30 40 50

Search:

40

Process:

Check 10 ❌

Check 20 ❌

Check 30 ❌

Check 40 ✅ Found

Search stops.

Linear Search Algorithm
Step 1: Start from index 0

Step 2: Compare current element with target

Step 3: If equal → Found

Step 4: Otherwise move to next element

Step 5: Repeat until end

Step 6: If not found → Element does not exist
topics.Output:
Found at Index 3

Topics Covered

✅ Topic 5: Array Traversal
✅ Topic 6: Array Input from User

Question 1

What is Array Traversal?

A. Creating an array

B. Updating an array

C. Visiting every element one by one

D. Deleting an array

✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation

Traversal means accessing every element of an array sequentially.

Question 2

Which loop is most commonly used for array traversal?

A. switch

B. if

C. for loop

D. try-catch

✅ Correct Answer: C

Question 3

A.

arr[i] = sc.nextInt();

B.

arr[i] = sc.next();

C.

arr[i] = input();

D.

arr.add();

✅ Correct Answer: A

Question 4

A.

B.

int sum = -1;

C.

int sum = 0;

D.

int sum;

✅ Correct Answer: C

Question 5

What is the correct formula for Average?

B. Sum - Number of Elements

C. Sum + Number of Elements

D. Sum ÷ Number of Elements

✅ Correct Answer: D

Question 6

For finding maximum element, the best initialization is:

A.

B.

int max = arr[0];

C.

int max = -1;

D.

int max = 100;

✅ Correct Answer: B

Question 7 (Output Prediction)

{
    10,

};

for(int num : numbers)
{
    System.out.println(num);
}

A.

10
20
30

B.

0
1
2

C.

30
20
10

D. Error

✅ Correct Answer: A

Question 8 (Output Prediction)

What will be the output?

int[] numbers =
{
    10,
    20,
    30,
    40
};

int sum = 0;

for(int num : numbers)
{
    sum += num;
}

System.out.println(sum);

A.

40

B.

100

C.

10

D. Error

✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation
10 + 20 + 30 + 40 = 100
Question 9 (Error Finding)

Find the error:

for(int i = 0; i <= arr.length; i++)
{
    System.out.println(arr[i]);
}

A. Missing loop

B. Wrong variable

C. Wrong condition

D. Missing semicolon

✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation

Correct:

i < arr.length

Using:

i <= arr.length

causes:

ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
Question 10 (Scenario Based)

A school system wants to find whether a roll number exists in an array.

Which algorithm should be used?

A. Bubble Sort

B. Linear Search

C. Nested Loop

D. Recursion

✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation

Linear Search checks elements one by one until the target is found.

topics.Common Mistakes:
Mistake 1

Forgetting break

Wrong:

if(arr[i] == target)
{
    found = true;
}

May continue unnecessary searching.

Mistake 2

Using == for Strings

Wrong:

name == target

Correct:

name.equals(target)
Mistake 3

Using index = 0

Wrong:

int index = 0;

If element isn't found:

0

looks valid.

Use:

int index = -1;
Mistake 4

Not Checking Result

Wrong:

System.out.println(index);

without verifying.

Better:

if(index != -1)
topics.Best Practices:
Use break After Finding

Good:

break;
Use -1 for Not Found

Good:

int index = -1;
Use equals() for Strings

Good:

name.equals(target)
Display Clear Messages

Good:

Found
Not Found
topics.Key Points:
Linear Search checks elements one by one.
Works on any array.
Time Complexity:
O(n)
Use:
boolean found

or

int index = -1
Use:
equals()

for String searching.
topics.Problem Statement:
Create an array:

10
20
30
40
50

Search for:

40

Display:

Found at Index 3
topics.Code Explanation:
The loop checks every element.

When:

numbers[i] == target

becomes true,

the current index is stored and the loop stops using:

break;

Output:

Found at Index 3
